frameworks.name: Drop Low or Go High
frameworks.definition: Digital marketing agencies serving SMBs must choose between two viable poles: ultra-cheap automated delivery (≤$400/month) or high-touch services for sophisticated business owners with proven metrics; the middle band ($1,500–$3,000/month) is a structural dead zone.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that SMB clients transfer their own operational volatility to the vendor: when an SMB's business struggles, they churn from the vendor's service, making SMB churn an inherent structural feature rather than a solvable service-quality problem.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: digital marketing services sold to SMBs
service-based SMB clients (cleaning, yard work, similar)
claims.mechanism: SMBs cannot sustain vendor spend when their own revenue declines; their operational dysfunction becomes the vendor's revenue dysfunction regardless of service quality.
bounded_by: Does not apply to enterprise clients with stable revenue cycles
Does not apply to SMBs locked into annual contracts with meaningful cancellation penalties

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that for digital marketing agencies serving SMBs at a price point of $1,500–$3,000 per month, average client stick (retention) is approximately 4–6 months.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

numbers.axis: average client stick length by price band for digital marketing services to SMBs

anchors.region: low-cost automated services at ≤$400/month (e.g., local SEO, review management)

bounded_by: Applies to Australian SMB market as described; may vary by market

claims.text: Alex Hormozi predicts that a digital marketing agency selling 10 new SMB clients per month at $2,000/month with an average stick of 5 months will plateau at approximately $100,000 per month in revenue and then stall, because churn offsets new sales at that volume and price point.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: At steady state, monthly new revenue (10 x $2,000 = $20,000) is offset by churning cohorts from prior months; the portfolio equilibrates near the LTV ceiling rather than compounding.

numbers.axis: monthly recurring revenue ceiling under these unit economics

anchors.value: stall — churn rate equals new sales rate

bounded_by: Does not apply if churn rate improves materially (e.g., longer-tenure clients)
Does not apply if sales velocity increases beyond 10/month before plateau is reached

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that customer acquisition cost (CAC) for an early-stage service business is never cheaper than at the moment the business launches, and will rise monotonically over time as the easiest-to-reach prospects are exhausted.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: CAC will never be cheaper than it is today
conditions: service business in early growth phase (0–6 months)
claims.mechanism: Early growth taps the founder's warmest personal and referral network; as that pool depletes, subsequent acquisition requires paid or cold channels which carry higher cost.
bounded_by: Does not apply to businesses that launch with a paid media channel already optimized
Does not apply if brand equity compounds virality faster than the warm-network depletes

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that a digital marketing agency serving SMBs at mid-market price points ($1,500–$3,000/month) will experience simultaneous margin compression and rising CAC, ultimately producing a non-profit-like feel despite growing revenue: the owner must keep selling simply to offset churn, without meaningful profit accumulation.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Rising CAC and persistent SMB churn force the owner to hire more staff to serve replacement clients while spending more to acquire them, compressing margins from both sides simultaneously.
bounded_by: Does not apply if the agency pivots to a more sophisticated client base with longer retention
Does not apply to low-cost automated models where delivery cost is near zero

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the only two viable business models for digital marketing agencies targeting SMBs are: (1) ultra-cheap automated delivery at $400/month or less, where the delivery cost is near zero and stick rates reach 30–40 months, or (2) high-touch services for sophisticated operators who already have proven sales processes and know their metrics, at premium price points.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: At the low end, near-zero delivery cost offsets high churn via volume and automated scale; at the high end, sophisticated clients stay longer and pay enough to fund high-touch delivery. The middle band fails because delivery cost is too high to survive SMB churn and price is too low to fund the client success effort required.

numbers.axis: viable price bands for digital marketing agency serving SMB-adjacent market

anchors.region: ultra-cheap automated (e.g., local SEO, review management)

anchors.value: premium price (unspecified), longer tenure, must have proven metrics and sales process

bounded_by: Applies to service-based business verticals in Australia as described; premium pricing tier not quantified in transcript
Does not apply to SaaS or product businesses with different churn dynamics

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the root cause of failure for digital marketing agencies that try to help struggling SMBs is that the SMB client does not have a functioning sales process or business fundamentals; the agency is being expected to solve a problem the client has not solved themselves.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: they don't know how to run their business
conditions: SMB clients in early stage without documented sales processes
claims.mechanism: Marketing amplifies what already exists in the business; if the SMB has no sales process, better lead flow produces chaos rather than revenue, leading to client dissatisfaction and churn even when the agency delivers qualified leads.
bounded_by: Does not apply to SMBs with a proven, repeatable sales process who just need more leads

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that low-cost automated digital marketing services (e.g., local maps ranking, review management, local SEO) priced at $300–$500/month achieve 30–40 month average stick rates from SMB clients, making their LTV comparable to higher-priced services despite the lower monthly fee.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: digital marketing services with near-zero delivery cost
service types: local maps ranking, review management, local SEO
claims.mechanism: Low-cost automated services are sticky because they produce visible, attributable results (e.g., top-3 maps placement) and require no ongoing effort from the client; cancellation requires an active decision against an immediately visible benefit.

numbers.axis: average client retention (stick) by price band and delivery model

anchors.region: automated low-cost ($300–$500/month, local SEO / review management / maps ranking)

bounded_by: Assumes near-zero ongoing delivery labor cost; stick rate advantage disappears if delivery requires significant manual effort per client

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that a digital marketing agency targeting the low-price automated model will still face a customer acquisition cost challenge: a large initial setup cost must be offset by a long-tail of small monthly recurring payments, so the economics only work if CAC stays low.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: A one-time setup fee (big head) plus a small monthly recurring fee (long tail) requires volume to generate acceptable returns; if CAC rises, the unit economics collapse because the margin per month is too small to absorb higher acquisition cost.
bounded_by: Does not apply to high-touch premium model where monthly fee is large enough to absorb higher CAC

claims.text: Alex Hormozi predicts that an SMB digital marketing agency growing quickly through strong sales will stall at approximately $3 million annual revenue unless it either restructures toward a lower-cost automated model or pivots to a higher-quality client avatar, because mid-market SMB churn creates an equilibrium ceiling below that revenue figure.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: you'll get to three, ten will suck
conditions: strong sales motion but no model change
claims.mechanism: SMB churn absorbs increasing portions of new sales revenue as the client base grows; the business can reach a local maximum near $3M but cannot compound past it without either reducing churn structurally (better avatar) or reducing delivery cost (automation).

numbers.axis: revenue ceiling for SMB-focused digital marketing agency without model change

anchors.value: requires model pivot (go low or go high)

bounded_by: Applies to agencies with strong sales but unresolved SMB churn
Does not apply if the agency successfully transitions avatar to sophisticated operators before hitting the ceiling

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the mid-market price band for digital marketing services to SMBs ($1,500–$3,000/month) is a structural dead zone where both the low-cost volume model and the premium high-touch model are economically viable, but this middle position is not, because delivery cost is too high relative to churn-adjusted LTV.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: in the middle is just a dead zone where everyone dies
claims.mechanism: The mid-market price band requires substantial delivery effort (ruling out the automation economics of the low tier) but serves clients too volatile and unsophisticated to justify the longer sales cycle and higher-touch retention investment of the premium tier.
bounded_by: This claim is specific to digital marketing services; mid-market pricing may be viable in other service categories with lower churn dynamics

examples.text: A digital marketing agency founder in Australia described his business: four months old, zero to $500k revenue, selling complete digital marketing services to SMBs (cleaning companies, yard work businesses) averaging $500k–$2.5M annual revenue, at $450/week (~$2,000/month) per client, acquiring roughly 10 new clients per month. Alex Hormozi used this exact situation to illustrate the structural ceiling: at 10 sales/month and a 5-month average stick, the portfolio would plateau near $100,000/month and then stall as churn offsets new client acquisition. The founder's strong early growth masked the unit-economics problem that would emerge at scale.

Alex Hormozi gave the example of local maps ranking (top-3 placement in Google Maps for a local area) and review management or local SEO as the canonical low-cost automated services that achieve 30–40 month stick rates at $300–$500/month. He contrasted this with the $2,000/month full-service digital marketing subscription, which generates only 4–6 month average stick, arguing the LTV is comparable but the CAC stays much lower in the automated model because of higher close rate (sales velocity) due to the lower price point.

Hormozi described the psychological trap he had observed across marketing agency founders he had worked with: as churn grows and margins compress, the founder begins to believe the solution is to take on more responsibility for the SMB client's entire business — building sales motions and nurturing sequences for the client's own customers. He cited this as a wrong path that compounds cost and complexity without solving the structural churn problem, because the agency is still serving a client who does not have a working business.

practices.text: If building a digital marketing agency targeting SMBs, choose one of two models before acquiring significant clients: (1) ultra-cheap automated delivery at ≤$400/month with near-zero labor cost per client, or (2) high-touch services for sophisticated operators with proven sales processes and business metrics. Do not compete in the mid-market price band ($1,500–$3,000/month) for unsophisticated SMBs.

When evaluating a digital marketing client prospect, require evidence that they already have a working sales process and know their own business metrics before signing them; avoid clients who expect the agency to solve a business problem they have not solved themselves.

For an agency pursuing the low-cost automated model, structure pricing as a one-time setup fee (big head) plus a small monthly recurring fee (long tail) and keep CAC low through high sales velocity at the lower price point.
